meta.topic: Be to + infinitive; be about to + infinitive

step_1_context.text: Be to + infinitive is commonly used in news reports to talk about events that are likely to happen in the near future:

step_2_explanation.simple_rule:
Be to + infinitive is commonly used in news reports to talk about events that are likely to happen in the near future:
 • Police officers are to visit every home in the area.
step_2_explanation.formula:
We often use be to + infinitive in if-clauses to say that something must happen first (in the main clause) before something else can happen (in the if-clause):
 • If the human race is to survive, we must look at environmental problems now.
step_2_explanation.common_mistake:
We use be about to + infinitive mainly in conversation to say that something will (not) happen in the very near future:
 • We're about to eat.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 12.1.1 Gil Moritz has written his first new play for 15 years. Its first performance ___ (stage) at the New Victoria Theatre.
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Formal announcement of a planned/controlled future event → be to + infinitive (passive).

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 12.1.2 The new safety system ___ (stop) trains automatically if they pass a danger signal.
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Official/formal arrangement or instruction → be to + infinitive.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 12.1.3 Stafford Boys' School ___ (merge) with the nearby Bicton Girls' School to form a new co-educational establishment.
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Official planned event reported as news → be to + infinitive (passive).

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 12.1.4 There are fears that sea levels ___ (rise) catastrophically in the next 50 years.
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Prediction about an uncontrollable natural event → will (not be to).

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 12.1.5 The old design and technology programme ___ (replace) with a new computer science course.
